title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted file attachments are definitely something we want to get to the bottom of quickly.

A few things to start narrowing this down: **Did PDF uploads work correctly before, and if so, when did you first notice the corruption?** Also, is this happening with *every* PDF you upload, or only certain ones (e.g., larger files, scanned documents, specific PDF versions)? And when you say the file is "damaged" — are you downloading it back from the task and then opening it, or is the preview within TaskFlow itself broken?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah it was working fine before, this started maybe a week ago? And no, not every PDF — I've noticed smaller ones seem to be okay actually. But the bigger reports our customers send are all coming through corrupted. We download them from the task and they just won't open. Look, we really need this fixed, our support team is dealing with angry customers every day over this.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the quick response — I understand the urgency and I'm sorry your support team is bearing the brunt of this.

To help us zero in on the cause: **do you have a rough sense of the file size cutoff?** For example, have you seen a PDF around 5 MB work fine while a 10 MB one comes through corrupted? If you can test with a couple of files of different sizes and tell me the approximate threshold, that would be extremely helpful.

Also, when the corrupted file fails to open, **what's the exact error message** from your PDF viewer (e.g., Adobe Acrobat, Preview, Chrome's built-in viewer)? And is the downloaded file the same size as the original, or noticeably smaller/larger?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't know the exact size cutoff, I haven't sat here testing different file sizes — I just know the little one-page PDFs seem fine and the bigger ones with like 10-20 pages are all broken. I don't remember the exact error, something about the file being damaged or not a valid PDF. I haven't compared file sizes, I'm not going to ask my support reps to start doing QA testing for you. Can you just look at the server logs or something? This broke about a week ago so something must have changed on your end.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Large PDF attachments corrupted on download — size-dependent regression introduced ~1 week ago","problem":"PDF files attached to tasks are downloaded in a corrupted/unreadable state. The corruption is size-dependent: small single-page PDFs work correctly, while larger multi-page PDFs (10-20 pages) consistently fail. The PDF viewer reports the file as damaged or not a valid PDF. This is a regression — uploads were working correctly until approximately one week ago. Multiple customers are affected and the support team is fielding daily complaints.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A change deployed approximately one week ago likely introduced a file-size-dependent corruption in the upload or download pipeline. Prime suspects: (1) a chunk size or buffer limit change in the upload handler that truncates or corrupts files above a threshold, (2) a misconfigured multipart upload boundary, (3) a streaming/encoding change (e.g., incorrect Content-Transfer-Encoding or premature stream termination), or (4) a storage layer change (e.g., S3 multipart upload configuration, CDN caching rule) that corrupts large objects.","reproduction_steps":["Upload a small (1-page, <1 MB) PDF to a task — confirm it downloads and opens correctly","Upload a larger (10-20 page, likely >5 MB) PDF to a task","Download the larger PDF from the task","Attempt to open the downloaded file — expect 'file is damaged' or 'not a valid PDF' error","Compare the byte size of the uploaded original to the downloaded file to determine if truncation is occurring"],"environment":"Production environment. No specific browser/OS reported — likely affects all clients since the corruption occurs server-side during upload or storage. Regression window: approximately one week prior to report (late March / early April 2026).","severity":"high","impact":"Multiple customers are unable to use PDF attachments for larger documents, which is a core workflow for the support team. The support team is handling daily escalations from angry customers. No workaround exists for affected files — users cannot upload large PDFs at all.","recommended_fix":"1. Review deployment history for changes made approximately one week ago, focusing on file upload/download handlers, storage configuration, and any infrastructure changes (CDN, proxy, storage backend). 2. Compare the byte content of an uploaded large PDF against the stored version and the downloaded version to identify where corruption is introduced (upload path vs. storage vs. download path). 3. Check for truncation — if the downloaded file is smaller than the original, look for buffer size limits, chunk size changes, or premature stream closure. 4. If file sizes match, inspect for encoding issues (binary vs. base64 mismatches, Content-Type handling). 5. Check server logs for errors or warnings during large file uploads in the past week.","proposed_test_case":"Upload PDFs of varying sizes (1 MB, 5 MB, 10 MB, 25 MB) and verify that each downloaded file is byte-identical to the original. Include a regression test that uploads a multi-page PDF above the observed failure threshold and asserts the downloaded content matches via checksum comparison.","information_gaps":["Exact file size threshold where corruption begins (can be determined via internal testing)","Whether the downloaded file size differs from the original (truncation vs. encoding corruption — can be checked server-side)","Exact error message from PDF viewers (minor — corruption type can be determined by inspecting the file bytes)","Whether the stored file on the backend is already corrupted or only the downloaded copy (determines upload-path vs. download-path issue)"]}
